bare: - Clark, E. V. (2014). "Common Ground." In B. MacWhinney & W. O'Grady (Eds.), *The Handbook of Language Emergence*. Wiley. [Stanford PDF](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs379c/class_messages_listing/curriculum/Annotated_Readings/ClarkCOMMON-GROUND-15_Unannotated.pdf)
claim_context: Cited as a reference in the common-ground reading list for establishing how speakers and listeners coordinate shared information over the course of a conversation.
source_says: Eve V. Clark's chapter synthesizes research on how common ground is built, signaled, and updated during language acquisition and interaction, drawing on Herbert Clark's grounding framework.
claim_support: Directly supports the chapter's use as a foundational pragmatic reference on common ground; the chapter is a canonical review of the topic.

bare: - **Clayman, S. (2001).** "Answers and Evasions." — Question-response sequences in political interviews. [CUP (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1017/S0047404501003037)
claim_context: Cited in the questions chapter as evidence that political interviews exhibit systematic question-response sequences where interviewees employ evasion strategies.
source_says: Clayman (2001, Language in Society) analyzes how politicians shift, resist, or evade interviewer questions, and how journalists pursue answers; the paper is foundational for the CA study of evasion.
claim_support: The paper is the canonical CA analysis of political question evasion, directly supporting the hub's framing.

bare: - Clayman, S. & Heritage, J. (2002). *The News Interview: Journalists and Public Figures on the Air*. Cambridge University Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/News-Interview-Journalists-Public-Figures/dp/0521002885)
claim_context: Cited in the topic-management reading list as a reference work on how journalists control topic in broadcast news interviews.
source_says: Clayman & Heritage's monograph is the definitive CA analysis of news-interview turn-taking, question design, and topic management.
claim_support: Canonical work on question/topic management in institutional talk; directly supports the citation.

bare: - Clift, R. (2001). "Meaning in interaction: The case of actually." *Language*, 77(2), 245-291. [JSTOR (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1353/lan.2001.0074)
claim_context: Hub cites as the landmark study on 'actually' as a marker of counter-expectation and gives Clift's finding that ~79% of sentence-initial 'actually' signals discrepancy.
source_says: Clift's Language paper analyzes 'actually' in a corpus of ordinary conversation, showing it functions to mark departures from projected expectation, especially in initial position.
claim_support: Clift is the canonical source for the counter-expectation analysis of 'actually'; the hub's characterization matches the paper.

bare: - Cloitre, M., Koenen, K. C., Cohen, L. R., & Han, H. (2002). claim_context: Cited as the original STAIR treatment outcome paper, supporting the hub's claim that emotion-regulation skills training precedes trauma processing and includes an assertiveness/interpersonal module.
source_says: Cloitre et al. (2002) is the first RCT of STAIR for childhood-abuse PTSD, reporting improvements in affect regulation, interpersonal skills, and PTSD symptoms in a phase-based treatment.
claim_support: Primary source for the STAIR protocol and its phase-based approach; matches the hub's framing.

bare: - [Cola et al. (2022) - Conversational adaptation](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9167260/)

bare: - Colich, N. L., et al. (2012). Individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorders Do Not Use Social Stereotypes in Irony Comprehension. PMC3991690.

source_says: The PMC3991690 paper is actually Zalla, Amsellem, Chaby, Sirigu, & Leboyer (2014), PLOS ONE — not Colich 2012. The paper's findings (stereotype does not spontaneously modulate irony detection in HFA/AS) do match the hub's claim.
claim_support: The claim is supported by the actual paper at PMC3991690, but the attribution is wrong: author is Zalla et al., year 2014, not Colich 2012.
notes: CITATION ERROR: The PMC ID points to Zalla et al. (2014) 'Individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorders Do Not Use Social Stereotypes in Irony Comprehension,' PLOS ONE 9(4):e95568. The claim itself is supported but author and year should be corrected to Zalla et al. 2014.

bare: - **Crompton et al. (2020)**: Autistic people share information with other autistic people **as effectively as** neurotypical people do with each other. In mixed groups, transfer drops. The communication system works; the **mismatch** is the problem.

bare: **Crompton et al. (2020)** demonstrated this experimentally: "Autistic people share information with other autistic people as well as non-autistic people do with other non-autistic people." Information transfer degraded only in mixed autistic/non-autistic groups, and rapport was higher in same-neurotype pairs (*Autism*, 24(7), 1901-1912).
claim_context: Narrative description of the diffusion-chain and rapport findings.
source_says: Same Crompton 2020 diffusion-chain paper.
notes: Pagination in this entry is wrong (1901-1912 vs correct 1704-1712).

key: debrabander|2019|thin slice work ran the study
bare: DeBrabander et al.'s (2019) thin-slice work ran the study in the opposite direction: when autistic raters evaluated thin-slice videos of autistic speakers, they did not show the negative first-impression bias that non-autistic raters showed. The autistic raters were rating the same speakers more accurately, or at least more charitably, because they were not bringing the non-autistic frame-repertoire to the task.
claim_context: In-text narrative version of the same DeBrabander 2019 finding.
source_says: Same source.

notes: Journal title may actually be 'Journal of English Linguistics' rather than 'Language in Society' — DOI 00754242 is the JEL prefix, not Language in Society. Minor metadata issue.

bare: Depraetere and Kaltenbock (2023) identify two main functions for hedged performatives: **downtoner** (reducing force) and **emphasizer** (adding force through apparent hedging). They play an important role in rapport management, serving mainly positive face politeness strategies.

bare: 7. **Dingemanse, M., Roberts, S.G., et al. (2015)**. Universal principles in the repair of communication problems. *PLOS ONE*, 10(9), e0136100. -- 12-language study of universal repair patterns. [PLOS](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0136100)

key: doherty|2015|quarmby k and other uk disabil
bare: - **Doherty, P. (2015), Quarmby, K., and other UK disability rights advocates** have written extensively on the mate crime pattern.
claim_context: Cited as advocacy/writing on the 'mate crime' pattern — where people befriend disabled individuals to exploit them.
source_says: UK disability-rights literature on 'mate crime' is well-documented, particularly by Katharine Quarmby and others; 'Doherty, P. (2015)' is less precise and may refer to Paul Doherty's work on disability hate crime.
claim_support: The general claim about UK mate-crime literature is supported by the broader field, but this citation is underspecified.
notes: Cannot verify a specific Doherty 2015 publication on mate crime. Quarmby's 'Scapegoat: Why We Are Failing Disabled People' (2011) is the canonical source. Consider replacing with a specific Quarmby cite.

source_says: Doherty et al. (2022, BMJ Open 12(2):e056904) is the cross-sectional survey of 1,248 autistic adults documenting healthcare barriers, diagnostic overshadowing, and adverse outcomes.

bare: - **Doherty et al. (2022)** on disclosure in healthcare settings, documenting that autistic adults often cannot safely disclose their diagnosis to clinicians without triggering diagnostic overshadowing — a direct pragmatic illustration of disclosure-as-stance-assignment.
claim_context: Applies Doherty 2022 to the disclosure/stance-assignment claim in identity/stance chapter.
source_says: Same source; the paper documents that many autistic adults report diagnostic overshadowing after disclosure.

bare: - Douglas & Sedgewick (2024). *Autism* 28(7):1732-1745. — [PMC11191373](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11191373/)
claim_context: Short-form citation of the Douglas & Sedgewick 2024 weaponized-features qualitative study.
source_says: Douglas & Sedgewick (2024, Autism 28(7):1732-1745, PMC11191373) interviewed 24 autistic adults about interpersonal victimization; almost all reported repeated IPV/sexual assault; core theme was 'autism used against you' — the weaponized-features framing.
claim_support: Directly supports the hub's weaponized-features framing.

bare: - **Douglas & Sedgewick (2024)**: Domestic violence prevalence among autistic adults: 23.7%. Physical violence: 56-58.5%. Sexual violence: 31-56.8%. Stalking and harassment: 58.5%. Published in *Autism*, 28(7).
claim_context: Cites Douglas & Sedgewick 2024 with specific prevalence percentages for DV, physical violence, sexual violence, and stalking.
source_says: The Douglas & Sedgewick 2024 paper is a small-N qualitative study (N=24) reporting that almost all participants had experienced repeated IPV and/or sexual assault; it cites prior meta-analytic prevalence numbers (40% sexual victimization, 84% multiple categories, 60-90% in women).
claim_support: The general claim is supported, but the specific percentages cited (23.7%, 56-58.5%, etc.) appear to be from studies cited within the Douglas & Sedgewick review, not primary findings of the study itself.
notes: Prevalence percentages attributed to Douglas & Sedgewick may actually trace to other studies the paper reviews (e.g., Griffiths et al., Pecora et al.). Verify primary attribution before press.

bare: - Douglas, S., & Sedgewick, F. (2024). "Experiences of interpersonal victimization and abuse among autistic people." *Autism* 28(7): 1732–1745. PMC11191373. (Weaponized features framework: autism-related traits used as components of manipulation tactics, including concern-framed moves.)
claim_context: Primary load-bearing citation for the manipulation hub: the weaponized-features framework and autistic interpersonal-victimization themes.
source_says: Douglas & Sedgewick (2024, Autism 28(7):1732-1745, PMC11191373, DOI 10.1177/13623613231205630) qualitatively interviewed 24 autistic adults, identifying six themes including 'autism used against you' (weaponized features) — abusers deliberately triggering meltdowns, exploiting diagnosis for discrediting, leveraging 'you get things wrong' messaging.
claim_support: Primary source for the weaponized-features framework; directly supports the core manipulation-hub framing.

bare: Douglas & Sedgewick (2024), in a qualitative interview study of 24 autistic adults, identified six themes in autistic people's experiences of interpersonal victimization. One of those themes — **autism used against you** — became the core of their conceptual contribution: the *weaponized features* framing.
claim_context: In-text narrative description of the Douglas & Sedgewick 2024 qualitative study and weaponized-features theme.
source_says: Same source; study described has N=24 autistic adults, six themes, 'autism used against you' as core.

bare: Douglas & Sedgewick (2024) introduced the *weaponized features* framing as a conceptual contribution from autistic-led qualitative research. The argument: perpetrators of abuse against autistic people often appear to **deliberately** exploit autism-related features as components of their tactics. The features are not the cause of the abuse; the *exploitation* of the features is the tactic.
claim_context: In-text explication of the weaponized-features framing.
source_says: Same source.

bare: - Douglas & Sedgewick (2024). — [PMC11191373](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11191373/)
claim_context: Short-form ubiquitous duplicate pointer to Douglas & Sedgewick 2024.
source_says: Same source.
notes: Duplicate variant (heavy repetition across manipulation hub files).

bare: - **Douglas & Sedgewick's (2024) weaponized features framework** — *"autism used against you"* — applies structurally: the autistic person's willingness to extend trust to someone who has signaled autism-affirmation is itself a feature that can be weaponized. The credentials become a tool in the harmer's kit rather than a reliable indicator of safety.
claim_context: Applies Douglas & Sedgewick 2024 weaponized-features framework to the performative-allyship pattern.
source_says: Same source.

bare: - Dovidio, J.F. & Ellyson, S.L. (1982). "Decoding Visual Dominance: Attributions of Power Based on Relative Percentages of Looking While Speaking and Looking While Listening." *Social Psychology Quarterly*, 45(2), 106-113. [JSTOR (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.2307/3033933)
claim_context: Cited as the foundational paper introducing the Visual Dominance Ratio (VDR) as a gaze-based measure of power attribution.
source_says: Dovidio & Ellyson (1982, Social Psychology Quarterly 45(2):106-113) is the canonical source for VDR — looking-while-speaking vs. looking-while-listening as an index of social dominance.
claim_support: Canonical source; directly supports the citation.
